blocks.text: you are snared by the utterance of your lips, caught by the words of your mouth.
So do this, my child, and save yourself, for you have come into your neighbor's power: go, hurry, and plead with your neighbor.
Give your eyes no sleep and your eyelids no slumber;
save yourself like a gazelle from the hunter, like a bird from the hand of the fowler.

1–5 : Caution against providing surety (i.e., guaranteeing loans). Proverbs regards the practice as too risky, particularly when the debtor is a stranger (11.15; 17.18; 20.16; 22.26–27; but cf. Sir 8.13).
Go to the ant, you lazybones; consider its ways, and be wise.
Without having any chief or officer or ruler,
it prepares its food in summer, and gathers its sustenance in harvest.
6–8 : , one of many proverbs that teach by observing the natural world (e.g., 30.18–19,24–28,29–31; Job 12.7–9; cf. 1 Kings 4.33).
How long will you lie there, O lazybones? When will you rise from your sleep?
A little sleep, a little slumber, a little folding of the hands to rest,
and poverty will come upon you like a robber, and want, like an armed warrior.
A scoundrel and a villain goes around with crooked speech,
winking the eyes, shuffling the feet, pointing the fingers,
13 : Body language reveals character. , i.e., regarding others with envy or hatred (Ps 35.19), or squinting while scheming trouble (10.10; 16.30).
with perverted mind devising evil, continually sowing discord;
on such a one calamity will descend suddenly; in a moment, damage beyond repair.
12–15 : Caution about the , a person of questionable character.
There are six things that the Lord hates, seven that are an abomination to him:
haughty eyes, a lying tongue, and hands that shed innocent blood,
17 : indicate arrogance (e.g., 21.4; 30.13). , i.e., commit murder (e.g., Deut 19.10–13; 21.1–9; Jer 7.5–7). , see 2.2n.
a heart that devises wicked plans, feet that hurry to run to evil,
a lying witness who testifies falsely, and one who sows discord in a family.
6.1–19: Admonitions.
16–19 : , a numerical proverb (see 30.1–33n.) about to God (see 3.32n.).
My child, keep your father's commandment, and do not forsake your mother's teaching.
Bind them upon your heart always; tie them around your neck.
21 : On Wisdom as adornment, see 1.9n.
When you walk, they will lead you; when you lie down, theya will watch over you; and when you awake, theya will talk with you.
22 : , lit., “she.” Parental instructions are personified as a woman who cares for her companion constantly (e.g., 4.6).
For the commandment is a lamp and the teaching a light, and the reproofs of discipline are the way of life,
23 : Teaching as a recalls the psalmist's similar description of God's word (Ps 119.105).
to preserve you from the wife of another, from the smooth tongue of the adulteress.
Do not desire her beauty in your heart, and do not let her capture you with her eyelashes;
25 : . A woman who “traps” her lover with her eyes and hair is a trope in ancient Near Eastern love poetry (e.g., Song 7.5; cf. Sir 26.9).
for a prostitute's fee is only a loaf of bread, but the wife of another stalks a man's very life.

26 : Comparison of a notably low (less than in Gen 38.17; cf. Sir 26.22) to the high cost of adultery suggests the warning is not about sex principally but the damage done by adultery to family and community, especially in patrilineal cultures (i.e., those tracing descent through the male line) like ancient Israel. Adultery raises questions about paternity and risks the passing of the family inheritance to illegitimate heirs. Deut 22.22 and Lev 20.10 designate adultery a capital offense.
Can fire be carried in the bosom without burning one's clothes?
Or can one walk on hot coals without scorching the feet?
So is he who sleeps with his neighbor's wife; no one who touches her will go unpunished.
29 : The “strange” woman (see 2.16n.) is described as “the evil woman” (see textual note , on the next page) and another's wife.
Thieves are not despised who steal only to satisfy their appetite when they are hungry.
Yet if they are caught, they will pay sevenfold; they will forfeit all the goods of their house.
But he who commits adultery has no sense; he who does it destroys himself.
29–32 : , an extreme penalty for a petty thief (cf. Ex 22.1–4,7).
He will get wounds and dishonor, and his disgrace will not be wiped away.
For jealousy arouses a husband's fury, and he shows no restraint when he takes revenge.
He will accept no compensation, and refuses a bribe no matter how great.
1.1–9.18: The proverbs of Solomon son of David, king of Israel.
6.20–35: Warning against adultery defined in ancient Israel as sex between an engaged or married woman and any man who is not her husband.
33–35 : drives the wronged husband to physically and unrelentingly attack the adulterer; see 27.4.
